APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue: it studies writing-style effects on a well-known NLP benchmark (ROC Story Cloze) using standard evaluation (task accuracy) and compares to prior models. Methods and evaluation meet community expectations for empirical analysis (logistic regression with interpretable stylistic features, analysis of discriminative features, reporting of hyperparameters), and the paper explicitly discusses limitations about coefficient interpretation. I deduct one point because code release is not mentioned and some baselines are only broadly referenced (e.g., "previous deep learning models" unspecified), which weakens reproducibility and clarity for top-tier standards. | B(5): This paper squarely targets core NLP research concerns—stylistic variation, dataset construction effects, and implications for machine reading—using standard experimental methodology (logistic regression with interpretable features) on a prominent benchmark (ROC Story Cloze). Its findings (style differences across writing tasks, a context-free stylistic classifier achieving strong/story-cloze results) are highly relevant to both modeling and dataset-design audiences at top venues. The problem formulation, evaluation (accuracy, human judgements), and positioning against prior work are appropriate for ACL/EMNLP/ICLR readers and provoke important methodological discussion about writing-task confounds.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue: it studies writing-style effects on a well-known NLP benchmark (ROC Story Cloze) using standard evaluation (task accuracy) and compares to prior models. Methods and evaluation meet community expectations for empirical analysis (logistic regression with interpretable stylistic features, analysis of discriminative features, reporting of hyperparameters), and the paper explicitly discusses limitations about coefficient interpretation. I deduct one point because code release is not mentioned and some baselines are only broadly referenced (e.g., "previous deep learning models" unspecified), which weakens reproducibility and clarity for top-tier standards.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper squarely targets core NLP research concerns—stylistic variation, dataset construction effects, and implications for machine reading—using standard experimental methodology (logistic regression with interpretable features) on a prominent benchmark (ROC Story Cloze). Its findings (style differences across writing tasks, a context-free stylistic classifier achieving strong/story-cloze results) are highly relevant to both modeling and dataset-design audiences at top venues. The problem formulation, evaluation (accuracy, human judgements), and positioning against prior work are appropriate for ACL/EMNLP/ICLR readers and provoke important methodological discussion about writing-task confounds.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper offers a useful empirical perspective by linking writing-task framings to measurable stylistic differences in the ROC Story Cloze data, which is a moderately new angle relative to prior Story Cloze and neural-baseline work (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016 and subsequent neural systems). However, the methods—logistic regression over standard stylistic features (char/word n-grams, POS, surface features)—are not new, and the core technical contributions are incremental rather than methodological innovations. The authors do clearly differentiate their contribution (analysis of task-induced style and feature importance, e.g., char n-grams effectiveness) from existing neural baselines, so the paper is informative but not highly novel. | B(3): The paper offers a clear and useful perspective—quantifying how different writing tasks (full stories vs. coherent/incoherent endings) induce measurable stylistic differences and showing that style-only classifiers can solve the Story Cloze Task—however this is largely an empirical, analytical contribution rather than a fundamentally new method. Prior work on the Story Cloze Task and on stylometry (e.g., Writeprints, neural baselines for Story Cloze) already explored related signal; the novel element is applying fine-grained style analysis to task framing and demonstrating strong performance with simple features (character and word n-grams). Overall the contribution is informative to experts (it surfaces a methodological confound and reproducible empirical findings) but is best characterized as a moderate, incremental advance rather than a breakthrough.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important methodological problem by demonstrating that different writing tasks induce measurable stylistic differences and that a simple style-only classifier achieves state-of-the-art on the Story Cloze Task, revealing a major dataset confound. From my perspective as a reproducible methodologist, these findings should change how researchers design and evaluate machine-reading benchmarks and prompt new controls or protocols to avoid task-framing artifacts. The work opens clear follow-on research directions (dataset construction, adversarial controls, style-robust models) and is likely to be widely cited and built upon within the NLP community, though its impact is primarily methodological rather than transformative across all of NLP. | B(4): This paper addresses an important methodological problem—how writing-task framing induces stylistic artifacts—which has direct consequences for evaluation in story understanding and related machine-reading tasks. Demonstrating that a simple style-based classifier (using character and word n-grams) outperforms prior deep models on the Story Cloze Task is likely to change how researchers interpret model successes and design datasets (e.g., controlling for authoring effects). The work opens clear new directions for dataset auditing, robustness testing, and style-aware model evaluation, and its findings are likely to be adopted by others constructing or analyzing crowdsourced narrative datasets. While impactful across the subfield, its influence is somewhat concentrated on dataset design and evaluation practices rather than producing a broadly generalizable modeling paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's primary empirical claim—that different writing tasks induce measurable stylistic differences and that a simple linear classifier on stylistic features can distinguish ending types and achieve strong Story Cloze performance—is supported by experiments on the ROC Story Cloze data and analysis of discriminative features. However, methodological details that matter for reproducibility and rigor are under-specified in the summary: code availability is not mentioned, baselines are only partially specified (previous deep models are unspecified), and no statistical significance testing is reported. The authors do acknowledge an important limitation about interpreting large logistic weights, but the state-of-the-art claim would be more convincing with clearer baseline comparisons and significance analysis. Overall the work is technically sound with minor issues in experimental completeness and reproducibility. | B(4): The paper’s main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing a simple logistic classifier with stylistic features can distinguish original/right/wrong endings and achieves state-of-the-art Story Cloze accuracy. There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate. Methodologically it is largely sound—reasonable baselines and feature analyses are reported—but the summary suggests missing details on statistical significance and potential confounds (e.g., rarity-driven large weights, fairness of comparison to context-aware deep models) that could affect interpretation. Overall the conclusions are plausible given the results, with only minor technical gaps that do not overturn the main findings.

However, methodological details that matter for reproducibility and rigor are under-specified in the summary: code availability is not mentioned, baselines are only partially specified (previous deep models are unspecified), and no statistical significance testing is reported. The authors do acknowledge an important limitation about interpreting large logistic weights, but the state-of-the-art claim would be more convincing with clearer baseline comparisons and significance analysis. Overall the work is technically sound with minor issues in experimental completeness and reproducibility.

Overall the conclusions are plausible given the results, with only minor technical gaps that do not overturn the main findings.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its simple stylistic classifier against several prior approaches (DSSM, LexVec and earlier deep learning models) on the same ROC Story Cloze data and task accuracy metric, which is appropriate and shows their method outperforming prior reported results. However, some important recent neural baselines that specifically target the Story Cloze Task (e.g., strong ending-focused neural models like "Pay Attention to the Ending" and the UW system) are not clearly enumerated or directly matched in evaluation detail, and a few baseline descriptions are left unspecified. Overall the comparisons are good and use the same data/metrics, but the paper could improve transparency and completeness by explicitly including and/or reproducing the strongest contemporaneous neural baselines and detailing any differences in evaluation conditions. | B(4): The paper compares against relevant prior Story Cloze Task systems (e.g., DSSM / Mostafazadeh et al.) and distributional baselines (LexVec), and it situates itself relative to prior neural approaches, claiming state-of-the-art performance. Comparisons appear to use the same ROC/Story Cloze data and task accuracy metrics, and the authors openly discuss when prior methods perform similarly and limitations of coefficient interpretation. However, some strong neural baselines (e.g., later attention-based models and UW system variants) are only generically referenced rather than directly instantiated or exhaustively compared, so while the evaluation is solid and fair overall, it omits a few specific high-performing competing systems.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its simple stylistic classifier against several prior approaches (DSSM, LexVec and earlier deep learning models) on the same ROC Story Cloze data and task accuracy metric, which is appropriate and shows their method outperforming prior reported results. Overall the comparisons are good and use the same data/metrics, but the paper could improve transparency and completeness by explicitly including and/or reproducing the strongest contemporaneous neural baselines and detailing any differences in evaluation conditions.

However, some strong neural baselines (e.g., later attention-based models and UW system variants) are only generically referenced rather than directly instantiated or exhaustively compared, so while the evaluation is solid and fair overall, it omits a few specific high-performing competing systems.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the introduction, methods, experiments, and conclusion follow a logical flow and the main contributions are stated early (differences in writing tasks, success of a style-based linear classifier, and feature findings). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and sufficiently detailed for reproduction (hyperparameters are reported and related work is thorough). Figures/tables and examples (e.g., feature analyses via logistic coefficients) are informative, though a few presentation gaps remain—some prior baselines are not specified in the summary and code availability is not mentioned—which slightly reduces reproducibility clarity. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions are stated early (differences in writing tasks and a style-based classifier achieving SOTA). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (logistic regression, feature sets, datasets, metrics) and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work and hyperparameter reporting. Figures/tables and examples seem informative enough to support analyses, though the discussion of limitations (e.g., interpretation of large coefficients) could be expanded for clarity, accounting for the minor presentation issues that prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the introduction, methods, experiments, and conclusion follow a logical flow and the main contributions are stated early (differences in writing tasks, success of a style-based linear classifier, and feature findings). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and sufficiently detailed for reproduction (hyperparameters are reported and related work is thorough). Figures/tables and examples (e.g., feature analyses via logistic coefficients) are informative, though a few presentation gaps remain—some prior baselines are not specified in the summary and code availability is not mentioned—which slightly reduces reproducibility clarity.

Notation and methodological description appear consistent (logistic regression, feature sets, datasets, metrics) and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work and hyperparameter reporting. Figures/tables and examples seem informative enough to support analyses, though the discussion of limitations (e.g., interpretation of large coefficients) could be expanded for clarity, accounting for the minor presentation issues that prevent a top score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents focused, well-executed experiments showing that simple stylistic classifiers separate original/right/wrong endings and even achieve strong Story Cloze accuracy, with analysis of top features (char n‑grams, word n‑grams) and acknowledgement of coefficient-interpretation limits. Related work appears thorough (places the study within Story Cloze literature and stylometry work) and hyperparameters are reported; they also discuss limitations of task framing. Missing, however, are deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., statistical significance, controls for rare-feature confounds, or more extensive ablations of model capacity and dataset splits) and code release is not mentioned, which weakens full reproducibility despite available datasets. | B(4): The experimental setup convincingly supports the core claims: classifiers using stylistic features distinguish original/right/wrong endings and attain state-of-the-art Story Cloze accuracy, with detailed feature analyses (e.g., character n-grams most predictive) and comparisons to prior systems (DSSM, LexVec, neural baselines). The paper includes sensible feature ablations (showing relative contributions of feature types) and reports hyperparameters, but it would be stronger with broader generalization tests (other corpora) and release of code for full reproducibility. Related work appears thorough and failure modes are discussed (notably limits of interpreting large logistic weights and the confound introduced by writing-task framing), so only minor gaps prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents focused, well-executed experiments showing that simple stylistic classifiers separate original/right/wrong endings and even achieve strong Story Cloze accuracy, with analysis of top features (char n‑grams, word n‑grams) and acknowledgement of coefficient-interpretation limits. Related work appears thorough (places the study within Story Cloze literature and stylometry work) and hyperparameters are reported; they also discuss limitations of task framing. Missing, however, are deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., statistical significance, controls for rare-feature confounds, or more extensive ablations of model capacity and dataset splits) and code release is not mentioned, which weakens full reproducibility despite available datasets.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental setup convincingly supports the core claims: classifiers using stylistic features distinguish original/right/wrong endings and attain state-of-the-art Story Cloze accuracy, with detailed feature analyses (e.g., character n-grams most predictive) and comparisons to prior systems (DSSM, LexVec, neural baselines). The paper includes sensible feature ablations (showing relative contributions of feature types) and reports hyperparameters, but it would be stronger with broader generalization tests (other corpora) and release of code for full reproducibility. Related work appears thorough and failure modes are discussed (notably limits of interpreting large logistic weights and the confound introduced by writing-task framing), so only minor gaps prevent a top score.

main_claims: Different writing tasks (writing full stories, writing coherent endings, writing incoherent endings) lead to measurable differences in authors' writing style.
A simple linear classifier using stylistic features (without using story context) can successfully distinguish among the three types of story endings.
A style-based classifier achieves state-of-the-art results on the Story Cloze Task, outperforming previous deep learning approaches.
Character n-grams are the most effective individual feature type for predicting the writing task, with word n-grams also capturing much of the signal.
_summary.methodology: Trained simple linear classifiers (logistic regression) using stylistic features (e.g., character n-grams, word n-grams, POS distributions, surface features) to distinguish original, right, and wrong story endings from the ROC Story Cloze data; analyzed most discriminative features via highest absolute logistic regression coefficients and compared against prior models.
datasets: ROC Story Cloze Task (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016a)
limitations: Interpretation of large logistic regression coefficients is unreliable since linear models can assign large weights to rare features (Yano et al., 2012).
Different writing-task framings can confound dataset design and affect writing style, posing a methodological challenge for machine reading tasks.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - writing style analysis / story cloze (machine reading)

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP models in both industry and academia, with emphasis on experimental rigor, reproducibility, and robust evaluation.
traits: insists on clear descriptions of datasets, splits, and preprocessing so experiments can be reproduced
looks for appropriate baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance when claims are empirical
examines feature representations and model capacity to ensure results are not due to incidental confounds
values clarity in hyperparameter tuning, implementation details, and runtime/compute reporting
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experimental design, baselines, and analyses support the empirical claims and whether the work can be reproduced and trusted.

reviewer_b.name: Narrative NLP Impact Analyst
reviewer_b.background: Experienced senior researcher in NLP with expertise in story understanding, writing-style analysis, and evaluation of methodological novelty and field impact across ACL/ICLR venues.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work and whether contributions advance understanding/theory
evaluates broader implications for downstream tasks (e.g., reading comprehension, generation) and dataset construction
scrutinizes interpretability analyses and whether insights are actionable for future research
considers potential failure modes, ethical implications, and limits of generalization beyond the studied corpus
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's novelty, significance, and how convincingly it situates itself within and advances the literature on story understanding and style analysis.
